course_title: Mathematics and Numeracy for Quality Teaching in the Primary Years

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a knowledge and a critical understanding of the importance of numeracy and mathematics and its relationship to contemporary issues in preparing children for full participation in community life, work and further education. (APST:1.2, 2.1, 2.5)
Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of research into how students learn mathematics and numeracy and the implications for teaching. (APST: 1.2, 2.1)

Critically analyse and solve a variety of mathematical tasks demonstrating knowledge and understanding of the mathematical concepts, substance and structures from different domains (including all strands of the mathematics curriculum and proficiencies) through application to real world contexts. (APST: 2.1, 3.4)

Appropriate subject matter expertise in the effective teaching of numeracy including the explicit teaching of mathematics and building of fluency, understanding, problem solving and reasoning. (APST: 2.1)

Demonstrate clear, fluent and coherent communication of mathematical thinking and reasoning using mathematical language including spoken, written and visual representations skills consistent with personal, professional and academic conventions in traditional and digital texts.

assessments.task_description:
Rationale

Primary teachers must have a breadth and depth of understanding of learning and teaching mathematics. As a teacher, you must be able to demonstrate and reflect on your understanding and skills of what and how students learn and build their numeracy skills across the strands, proficiencies and mathematical practices of the Australian Curriculum: Mathematics.

Task

For each Reflective Response, you will be given a prompt to respond to in writing in the workshop in Weeks 3, 6, 9, 11 and 13. The prompt will ask you to reflect on content in the course, focusing on the previous 2-3 weeks of workshops, assigned readings, fluency practice and written reflections.

These Reflective Responses are not meant to be tests of recall, but rather demonstration that you can reflect on and apply what you are learning, and that you are to explain your understanding by drawing on and discussing examples and making connections from the course.

Criteria

The responses will be graded as pass/fail and you must pass at least three of the five spaced responses offered in Weeks 3, 6, 9, 11 and 13 to pass the course.

You will be assessed on your ability to:

  1. Demonstrate knowledge of research into how students learn mathematics and numeracy and the implications for teaching (LO2)
  2. Demonstrate application of knowledge and a critical understanding of the importance of numeracy and mathematics in preparing children for full participation in personal and community life, work and further education (LO1)
  3. Appropriate subject matter expertise in the effective teaching of numeracy including the explicit teaching of mathematics and building of fluency, understanding, problem solving and reasoning. (LO4)
  4. Demonstrate communication skills (written and visual) for a critical evaluation and adherence to suitable text structures and linguistic features. This includes academic referencing (LO5)

Submission

Your submission will consist of the following:

Your responses to the prompt must be hand-written on the sheet provided during the workshop unless there is an accessibility requirement, completed individually in person and without access to electronic devices of any kind during the task. Any attempted use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Responses will be handed in to the lecturer or tutor.

You must bring your UQ Student ID to class.

By submitting your assignment, you are certifying that it is your original work. You need to ensure you have completed UQ’s academic integrity module before you submit your assignment. The module can be found at: https://www.uq.edu.au/integrity.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission will be via hard-copy on document provided by the instructor. Students must bring their UQ student ID card with them to class.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: AT2: Group Numeracy video: Real life maths
assessments.category: Creative Production/ Exhibition, Paper/ Report/ Annotation
assessments.task_description:
**BE SURE TO CAREFULLY READ THE CRITERIA IN THE TASK SHEET POSTED IN THE ASSESSMENT FOLDER IN BLACKBOARD**

Rationale: Teachers often use videos or other media as focus activities to stimulate student learning and engagement. The fact that you can make your own presentation media means that you can bring relevance from the real world into the classroom. You can also make the link between Mathematics and Numeracy more readily than in the past. In the classroom, teacher media production serves as an important model for students who are making their own media using devices such as iPads. Your use of digital media tools will allow you to develop technical and communication skills in critical and creative ways to expand curriculum learning opportunities for all students including those from diverse linguistic, cultural, religious and socioeconomic backgrounds. At the same time, you will develop knowledge and understanding of the kinds of teaching strategies needed for using ICT such as those involved in the use and production of presentation media to support and expand curriculum learning opportunities for students.

Task: For this assignment, you will work in a group of 2 to create a video, digital media or multi-media slide deck (e.g. PowerPoint) for a primary school aged audience that explores mathematics in a real-life scenario. This is not a lesson, but an exploration of mathematics in a real-life scenario. You are to upload with the research report a link to your video, digital media or multi-media slide deck.

Part A: The presentation (link to video, digital media or multi-media PowerPoint to be included with research report)

  • The video, media presentation or a multi-media slide deck (with 6-12 slides) that you and a partner produce is to be approximately 3 minutes duration (+ or - 30 seconds)
  • You must choose a real-life scenario that illustrates a numeracy context
  • The content of your presentation must explore the scenario and mathematics in it in a way that is suitable for an audience of children of primary school age.
  • Your presentation must be compatible for easy viewing and downloading for assessment.
  • This is not a ‘professional’ presentation! As sustainable teacher-produced media, it just has to be competently made in a timely fashion. The information has to be correct.

Part B: The Research Report

This task also requires you to individually produce a research brief (1000 words) in which you:

  • Make clear connections between the real-world context you selected and the numeracy in it.
  • Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of research into how students learn the mathematical concept you are exploring and the implications for teaching it through a real-life context.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
Week 1 - Week 12

Weekly throughout the semester. Each weekly submission is due the Friday of the following week. For example, the Week 4 submission is due on the Friday of Week 5.
assessments.task_description:
**BE SURE TO CAREFULLY READ THE CRITERIA IN THE TASK SHEET POSTED IN THE ASSESSMENT FOLDER IN BLACKBOARD**

Rationale: As a quality teacher, you need mathematical content knowledge prior to and beyond the level you teach. Teachers who have a deep understanding of and able to reflect on their mathematical content knowledge are more likely to recognise and maximise young students’ potential to learn. This will support students to develop a positive disposition towards mathematics, which has been identified as a key contributor to students’ success. For these reasons, this assessment will help you to develop your understanding of mathematical structures and models and support you with the development of the deep knowledge of the mathematics required to teach primary school students.

Task: Throughout the course, you are required to work on a weekly mathematical folio, which is your opportunity to demonstrate appropriate subject matter expertise in the effective teaching of numeracy including the explicit teaching and learning of mathematics and building of fluency, understanding, problem solving and reasoning. You also need to demonstrate your commitment to developing your mathematical disposition, understanding of the mathematical content and communication needed for teaching primary mathematics.

The folio consists of:

  • Part A: Activity notes and reflection (completed in class).
  • Part B: Notes on weekly text reading and annotated mathematical problem-solving that demonstrates mathematical disposition (curiosity, productive struggle), mathematical thinking and communication as well as personal reflection on your developing mathematical understanding and fluency.

You are to upload your weekly folio. Part A will be checked in class; if you are absent, then you must include the activity notes, photos and reflection with your upload for Part B. You can choose two folio weeks not to be counted over the semester (assumed as your lowest or missing submissions).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Weeks 1-3: Numeracy and mathematical disposition

In these introductory weeks, we will introduce numeracy and outline the critical importance of mathematical disposition for developing mathematical understanding, fluency, problem-solving and reasoning (mathematical proficiencies). In the first three workshops, drawing from the research, students learn the difference between numeracy and mathematics and how this is applied through learning, discourse, and critique across all areas of the curriculum.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Weeks 1-3: Numeracy and mathematical disposition In these introductory weeks, we will introduce numeracy and outline the critical importance of mathematical disposition for developing mathematical understanding, fluency, problem-solving and reasoning (mathematical proficiencies). learning_activities.topic:
Weeks 4-8: Number

We will focus these weeks on exploring mathematical reasoning in number.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Weeks 4-8: Number We will focus these weeks on exploring mathematical reasoning in number.

learning_activities.topic:
Weeks 9-10: Algebra, Measurement and Geometry

Algebra gives us language to reason about patterns and mathematical relationships. Measurement and Geometry allow us to develop spatial awareness and how we measure our world.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Weeks 9-10: Algebra, Measurement and Geometry Algebra gives us language to reason about patterns and mathematical relationships. Measurement and Geometry allow us to develop spatial awareness and how we measure our world.

learning_activities.topic:
Weeks 11-12: Probability & Statistics

Uncertainty is a normal part of life. These weeks will focus on how probability and statistics gives us a way to capture and make sense of uncertainty.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13: Bringing it all together

In the final week, we will draw on our understanding across the course to look at the world and celebrate where we have come.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
